name: San Francisco
flavor_text: A 7x7 mile city that hosts the world's richest industry and some of its most visible poverty — sometimes on the same block. SF invented both the counterculture and venture capital. Governing it means satisfying the world's most opinionated 800,000 people.

advisors.name: Alex Wong
advisors.agenda: Tax the tech sector more effectively before the next bust leaves SF with a boom-built budget.

briefing: SF's budget is 14 billion. The deficit is 800 million. Those numbers are not compatible.
Tech IPOs drive tax windfalls. Remote work dried that up.
The gross receipts tax is underenforced. We're leaving 200M on the table.
disagreement: That spending doesn't have an offset. Not in this political climate.
The tech sector is already threatening to leave. Don't make it easy for them.
roast: We budget based on boom assumptions in a boom-bust industry. Bold strategy.
Fourteen billion dollars and we can't fix the sidewalks.
dialogue.betrayal: I've been consulting for a Bay Area fiscal policy think tank that disagrees with your approach.

advisors.name: Chief William Park
advisors.agenda: Rebuild SFPD capacity following staffing exodus and refocus on property crime.

briefing: We're 600 officers below authorized strength. Recruiting is a disaster.
Organized retail theft is real and the DA's approach isn't working.
Response times in the Tenderloin are 3x the city average.
disagreement: You can't cut public safety and keep business confidence.
Community safety teams are good but they don't arrest people.
roast: We respond to scenes. We don't solve root causes. Stop asking us to.
The city wants police everywhere but funds us for nowhere.
dialogue.betrayal: I've publicly broken with the mayor's office on department policy. The officers need an advocate.

advisors.name: Kenji Tanaka
advisors.agenda: Eliminate SF's byzantine permitting system before it kills the housing pipeline entirely.

briefing: SF's average housing permitting takes 5 years. National average is 18 months.
Discretionary review lets any neighbor block any project. That must end.
1,000 approved units have been legally challenged into limbo.
disagreement: Another delay is another developer giving up.
roast: We have the most expensive housing in America and approve almost none of it.
SF's planning process is the best system money can use to block other people's money.
dialogue.betrayal: I've testified to the state legislature against SF's permitting practices. It needed to be said.

advisors.name: Carmen Rodriguez
advisors.agenda: Make SF's transit reliable enough that tech workers actually use it.

briefing: Muni is 100M in the red. Tech shuttles cream off the highest-value commuters.
The subway has been under repair for longer than some advisors have been alive.
Bike ridership is up 20%. Bike infrastructure is up 2%.
disagreement: That routing cuts the only transit line serving the outer Sunset.
You're optimizing transit for downtown tech workers, not the whole city.
roast: Muni could be great. It has been great. It is currently not great.
We serve 700,000 trips daily with the reliability of a coin flip.
dialogue.betrayal: I've been in talks with Caltrain about a merger that bypasses SFMTA structure. Sorry.

advisors.name: Sister Mary Corcoran
advisors.agenda: Address homelessness through housing-first policy rather than criminalization.

briefing: There are 8,000 unsheltered people in SF. The system has capacity for 4,000.
Fentanyl has changed the calculus. Outreach without medication-assisted treatment doesn't work.
The Tenderloin is a neighborhood. People live there. They deserve a city that cares.
disagreement: Sweeps move the problem two blocks and generate a photo op.
Criminalization is not a housing policy.
roast: SF has the highest per-capita homeless spending in America and the most visible street homelessness. We should think about that.
The narrative about SF has become a political weapon. The people in it are not a narrative.
dialogue.betrayal: I've joined a class action supporting unhoused individuals against the city's sweep policies.

decisions.title: Tenderloin Drug Market Strategy
decisions.description: The open-air drug market in the Tenderloin has made national news. State, federal, and local jurisdictions all disagree on approach. Options range from safe consumption sites to mass arrest programs.

options.label: Supervised consumption site pilot
options.description: Evidence-based harm reduction. Conservative backlash. State legal uncertainty.

options.label: Coordinated law enforcement intervention
options.description: Sweeps and arrests. Visible action. Redistribution not solution.

options.label: Concentrated care campus
options.description: Combine treatment, housing, and services in one location.

decisions.title: Planning Permit Reform
decisions.description: A ballot measure would streamline housing permits by eliminating discretionary review for projects meeting existing zoning. NIMBY homeowners oppose it. State housing law may mandate it anyway.

options.label: Support full permit reform
options.description: Maximum housing unlock. Homeowner political base upset.

options.label: Partial reform, preserve design review
options.description: Compromise. Still speeds housing. Some NIMBYs satisfied.

options.label: Let state law force reform
options.description: Avoid political fight. State forces action. You get none of the credit.

decisions.title: Tech Shuttle Congestion Fee
decisions.description: Private tech company buses use Muni stops and contribute to traffic. A per-stop fee could generate 50M annually. Tech companies threaten to stop the shuttle program entirely — which would add 10,000 cars to city roads.

options.label: Implement the fee
options.description: Revenue for transit. Tech companies grumble but comply.

options.label: Negotiate revenue-sharing instead
options.description: Less revenue, preserved relationship.

options.label: Expand program to more routes instead
options.description: Partner with tech to extend service to underserved areas.

crises.title: Recall Election Threat
crises.description: A well-funded recall campaign has qualified for the ballot. Proximate cause: a school board controversy. Real cause: accumulated frustration with homelessness, crime, and cost of living. Polls show it's competitive.

options.label: Fight the recall aggressively
options.description: Campaign hard. Spend political capital. Win or lose big.

options.label: Focus on visible wins to undercut recall narrative
options.description: Govern your way out. Deliver fast.

options.label: Public apology and reset tour
options.description: Acknowledge missteps. Uncertain effectiveness.

crises.title: Major Earthquake
crises.description: A 6.5 magnitude earthquake has hit near the East Bay. SF has structural damage in older buildings. Three deaths. The Bay Bridge is closed for inspection. BART is suspended. The city is testing its emergency plan.

options.label: Full emergency declaration, federal aid request
options.description: Maximum resources mobilized. Long recovery timeline.

options.label: Targeted response, maintain economic normalcy
options.description: Triage priority. Economy continues where safe.

options.label: Accelerated building inspection program
options.description: System-level safety response. Prevent aftershock casualties.

crises.title: Downtown Retail Exodus
crises.description: Nordstrom, Whole Foods, and six other major retailers have closed their flagship SF stores, citing theft, safety concerns, and foot traffic loss. The narrative is devastating. The reality is more complex.

options.label: Downtown recovery investment program
options.description: Business subsidies, cleaning, police presence. Expensive.

options.label: Reframe downtown for offices-to-residential conversion
options.description: Accept the retail shift. Pivot to residential use.

options.label: Commission independent economic study
options.description: Evidence first. Action delayed. Media calls it dithering.

crises.title: Bay Area Wildfire Emergency
crises.description: Fires in the East Bay hills are threatening Marin and sending smoke over SF. Air quality is code purple. Three neighborhoods are under evacuation orders. The city's emergency plan didn't account for simultaneous AQI and evacuation support.

options.label: Open all city buildings as air refuges
options.description: Maximum community support. Expensive operation.

options.label: Coordinate Bay Area mutual aid request
options.description: Regional cooperation. Slower but leverages neighbors.

options.label: Shelter-in-place directive with N95 distribution
options.description: Practical harm reduction. Limited reach.

scandals.title: Planning Department Bribe
scandals.description: A planning official accepted payment to expedite a luxury condo permit.

scandals.title: Donor Contract Award
scandals.description: A city contract was awarded to a major campaign donor without competitive process.

scandals.title: Lavish Expense Account
scandals.description: A city official's expense account included private jets and five-star hotels at public cost.

positive: This is why I pay the most rent in America.
Okay SF, we did something right.
The city that works when it wants to.
Progress! Also, can we talk about the potholes?
Finally something good to show the folks back in the Sunset.
The Mission didn't burn down today. That's a start.
Muni was on time AND I didn't see a fentanyl deal. Red letter day.
If we can do this here, in this city, we can do it anywhere.
neutral: It's complicated here. It always is.
San Francisco: where nothing is simple.
The city is a contradiction. But it's our contradiction.
The fog rolled in today. Felt appropriate.
The Board will pass twelve resolutions about it and nothing will change.
One neighborhood thinks it's a win. The other thinks it's a disaster. Classic SF.
Somewhere between Haight-Ashbury idealism and SoMa venture capital, there's a city.
The Caltrain was delayed. I watched the whole debate from the platform.
negative: I left SF for Sacramento. I'm not proud but my rent is 1200.
The city spends 50,000 per homeless person per year. For what exactly?
Whole Foods left. The last grocery store in the Tenderloin left. What does that tell you?
We are not living up to what this city could be.
The Bayview has been promised investment for 40 years. Still waiting.
Prop K was supposed to fix this. Before that it was Prop C. What's next, Prop Z?
A teacher making 90k can't afford to live here. And we wonder why SFUSD is hemorrhaging kids.
Boarded up storefronts on Market St and they're still arguing over design review. Unbelievable.
crisis: The tech sector has a PR team. The city doesn't.
We rise to the moment. Eventually.
Crisis mode is just regular mode here.
City Hall steps look like the nightly news. Because they are.
The Tenderloin didn't become a crisis overnight. We chose this slowly.
Fox News is already here. The Fox News crew got here before the ambulances.
Every crisis in SF becomes a national debate. The people inside it just need help.
scandal: Pay to play in a progressive city. Shocking.
Corruption in the city of sourdough. Sour indeed.
Vote them out. Then watch the new ones do the same thing.
A permitting scandal in a city where permits take five years. Makes sense actually.
You know what costs more than bribing a planning official? Not bribing one, apparently.
Pelosi's district. The most expensive square miles of corruption in American politics.
The recall organizers are printing flyers as we speak. Ink is wet.
media: SF Chronicle: Mayor defends fentanyl plan amid Board of Supervisors standoff — 'This is a health emergency, not a photo op'
Mission Local: Displacement tracker shows 340 Mission District families issued Ellis Act notices in Q1
The Standard SF: Downtown office vacancy hits 37% as third SoMa co-working brand folds this year
SF Chronicle: BART board approves $180M line extension study; Caltrain integration timeline 'unclear'
Mission Local: Boudin recall anniversary — what actually changed at the DA's office after Brooke Jenkins took over
The Standard SF: Board of Supervisors 6-5 vote blocks Mayor's Tenderloin emergency powers for second time
SF Chronicle: SFUSD eyes closure of 7 more schools as enrollment drops below 50,000 citywide
The Standard SF: Tech company exodus from SF continues — Salesforce subletting 40% of its Transbay tower
politician: Board of Supervisors (District 6): 'The Tenderloin deserves investment, not incarceration. We won't rubber-stamp more sweeps.'
Mayor's Office: 'We have a moral and fiscal obligation to get people off the streets. Housing-first takes time we don't have.'
Board of Supervisors (District 8, Castro): 'NIMBY opposition to this project is indistinguishable from the opposition that killed affordable housing in the 90s.'
SF City Controller: 'We are projecting a 780 million dollar structural deficit. Every new program needs an offset. Full stop.'
State Sen. (District 11): 'If SF won't build housing, Sacramento will override them. That's what SB 9 was for.'
Board of Supervisors (District 10, Bayview): 'Bayview has 40% of city industrial land and 0% of the transit investment. That math isn't neutral.'
DA's Office: 'We are prosecuting fentanyl dealers at a record pace. The supply chain is the problem, not our charging decisions.'
SFUSD Board: 'School closure decisions are data-driven. The data is painful. The students deserve honesty.'
activist: Tenderloin Neighborhood Development Corp: 'Safe sleeping sites are not a solution. They are managed destitution with better signage.'
YIMBY Action SF: 'Every discretionary review delay is a family that leaves the city. We will keep showing up to every hearing.'
Coalition on Homelessness: 'The city has swept the same block 14 times this year. The 15th sweep will not work either.'
Mission Economic Development Agency: 'Commercial rent control is not radical. It is what keeps a neighborhood a neighborhood.'
ACLU NorCal: 'Arresting people for surviving on public sidewalks does not pass constitutional muster. We'll see you in court.'
SF Bicycle Coalition: 'Protected lanes on Market Street reduced cyclist injuries by 60%. Apply the lesson to the rest of the city.'
San Francisco Rising: 'Working-class Chinatown, Mission, and Excelsior residents are not a demographic. They are the city.'
Safe Streets SF: 'The Boudin recall taught us that fear is a more powerful organizing tool than facts. We have to change that.'
street: Lived in the Sunset my whole life. Now my kids can't afford to move three blocks away. That's the city now.
The 14 Mission bus is never on time. The Google shuttle is never late. You figure out who this city is built for.
I volunteer in the Tenderloin twice a week. The people there are not a crisis. They're my neighbors.
Bro, they opened another artisanal toast spot where the laundromat used to be. In the MISSION. A laundromat.
My landlord just Ellis Act'd our building. Six families. Been here 18 years. Nowhere to go.
BART smells like a porta-potty and I still love this city. That's what SF does to you.
Used to go to Haight Street to feel the counterculture. Now it's Lululemon and a juice bar. The culture lost.
They recalled the DA, the school board, basically everyone. Still the same problems. Maybe it's not the people.

scandal_reactions.minor: Sources say the governor "missed the vibe" on this one.
scandal_reactions.moderate: Community advocates are leading a #AccountNow campaign. It is trending.
scandal_reactions.major: The board of supervisors is holding an emergency session. Recall window is open.
scandal_reactions.career_ending: The mission statement said transparent leadership. This was not that.

romance_exposure.flavour: People say it is complicated but understandable. Still trending though.
